title: Data Types Overview
subtitle: Discover the four basic types of data Python works with
learning_objectives: Name and describe the four basic Python data types: str, int, float, and bool
Use the type() function to check the type of any value or variable
Explain why data types matter and what happens when you mix them incorrectly
Understand what None represents in Python

sections.heading: The Four Basic Data Types
sections.content:
Every value in Python has a type — it tells Python what kind of data it is and what operations make sense for it. There are four basic types you will use constantly:

str (string) — Text. Any sequence of characters wrapped in quotes. Examples: "Hello", "Python", "123 Main Street". Even if a number is inside quotes like "42", it is still a string, not a number.

int (integer) — Whole numbers, positive or negative, with no decimal point. Examples: 7, -3, 1000, 0.

float (floating-point number) — Numbers that have a decimal point. Examples: 3.14, -0.5, 100.0. Even 100.0 is a float, not an int, because of the decimal point.

bool (boolean) — A value that is either True or False — nothing else. Booleans are used to represent yes/no or on/off situations. Note that True and False must be capitalized in Python.

You can use the built-in type() function to ask Python what type any value or variable is.
sections.code_example:
name = "Alice"       # str
age = 30             # int
height = 1.75        # float
is_student = True    # bool

print(type(name))
print(type(age))
print(type(height))
print(type(is_student))
sections.note: The type() function is extremely handy for debugging. When you are not sure what type a value is, just print(type(value)) to find out.

sections.heading: Why Types Matter — and What None Means
sections.content:
Data types are not just labels — they determine what you can do with a value. Python keeps types separate to avoid nonsensical operations. For example, you cannot add a number directly to a string because Python doesn't know if you want math or text joining. You would need to convert first.

This is actually a helpful protection — Python tells you immediately when you accidentally mix types, rather than silently producing a wrong answer.

There is also a fifth special value worth knowing: None. None represents the absence of a value — it means "nothing" or "empty." It is its own type (NoneType) and is different from zero, False, or an empty string. You will often see None used as a default value when something hasn't been set yet.
sections.code_example:
# Why types matter: mixing them carelessly causes errors
number = 5
word = "hello"

# This works -- adding two ints:
print(3 + 4)

# This works -- joining two strings:
print("hello" + " world")

# Converting a number to string to combine them safely:
print("I have " + str(number) + " apples")

# None represents no value
result = None
print(result)
print(type(result))
sections.code_output:
7
hello world
I have 5 apples
None
<class 'NoneType'>
sections.warning: If you try to run print("I have " + 5 + " apples") without converting 5 to a string first, Python will raise a TypeError. Always convert values to the right type before combining them.

exercises.title: Check the Types
exercises.prompt:
Create four variables, one of each basic type:
- A string variable called my_name
- An integer variable called my_age
- A float variable called my_height (use a decimal like 1.70)
- A boolean variable called am_learning (set it to True)

Then use print(type(...)) to print the type of each one.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a string variable called my_name and assign your name to it
# Create an integer variable called my_age and assign a number to it
# Create a float variable called my_height and assign a decimal number to it
# Create a boolean variable called am_learning and set it to True

# Print the type of each variable
exercises.solution:
my_name = "Taylor"
my_age = 22
my_height = 1.70
am_learning = True
print(type(my_name))
print(type(my_age))
print(type(my_height))
print(type(am_learning))
hints: An integer is a whole number: my_age = 22
A float has a decimal: my_height = 1.70
Use print(type(variable_name)) to check each type

exercises.title: Safe Type Conversion
exercises.prompt:
You have a number stored in a variable. Combine it with text safely by converting it to a string first.

1. Create a variable called num_languages and set it to 3
2. Print the sentence: I am learning 3 programming languages!

Use str() to convert the number to a string so you can join it with text using +.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a variable called num_languages and set it to 3

# Print the sentence using str() to convert the number
exercises.solution:
num_languages = 3
print("I am learning " + str(num_languages) + " programming languages!")
hints: You cannot directly add a number to a string with + — you must convert first.
Use str() to turn the number into a string: str(num_languages)
Then join the strings with +: "I am learning " + str(num_languages) + " programming languages!"

tests.message: Output contains: I am learning 3 programming languages!

quiz.question: What is the data type of the value: "99"  (with the quotes)?
options: int, because it is a number
float, because it could be a decimal
str, because it is inside quotes
bool, because it can be true or false
quiz.explanation: Even though 99 looks like a number, the quotes around it make it a string (str). In Python, anything inside quotes is treated as text, not a number. If you want the number 99, write it without quotes: 99

quiz.question: What does None represent in Python?
options: An empty string with no characters
The absence of any value — nothing at all
quiz.explanation: None is Python's way of saying "no value here." It is not the same as 0, False, or an empty string (""). None is its own special type (NoneType) and represents the complete absence of a value.

quiz.question: What does the type() function do?
options: It changes the type of a variable
It tells you what type a value or variable is
It converts a value to a string
It counts how many characters are in a value
quiz.explanation: type() is a built-in function that tells you what data type a value is. For example, type(3.14) returns <class 'float'> and type("hello") returns <class 'str'>. It is very useful for debugging.

quiz.question: Which of these values is a float?
quiz.explanation: 100.0 is a float because it has a decimal point. 42 is an int (no decimal point). "3.14" is a string (it has quotes). True is a boolean.

quiz.question: What error does Python raise when you try to add a number directly to a string using +?
quiz.explanation: Python raises a TypeError when you mix incompatible types in an operation — like trying to add an integer to a string with +. Python doesn't know if you want math or text joining, so it raises an error instead of guessing.

completion_message: You have completed Module 1! You now understand the four basic data types that are the building blocks of all Python programs. Knowing the difference between strings, integers, floats, and booleans — and how to check and convert them — will serve you incredibly well as you continue. You should be very proud of how far you have come!
